one_line_takeaway: CoSA improves black-box transferability of point-cloud adversarial attacks by restricting perturbations to a compact, prototype-guided low-rank latent subspace rather than the raw input or a model-specific feature space, beating 8 prior transfer attacks by roughly 8-15 ASR points across 4 architectures and 2 datasets, with ablations showing the semantic base subspace (not the low-rank perturbation subspace alone) drives most of the gain.
key_finding: CoSA generally achieves the highest black-box transfer attack success rate (ASR) among the nine compared methods across most source-target model pairs on both ModelNet40 and ScanObjectNN and both l_infinity budgets (0.18, 0.45), typically outperforming the strongest baseline CFG by roughly 8-12 percentage points and up to about 15 points on harder targets (PCT, PointMamba) -- e.g. PointNet(surrogate)->PointMamba at eps=0.45: CoSA 65.6% vs. CFG 56.8%; DGCNN(surrogate)->PointMamba at eps=0.45: CoSA 75.9% vs. CFG 72.5%. Under four defenses (Table II, PointNet as source/target), CoSA gives the best white-box ASR under SRS/DUP-Net/AT (e.g. SRS eps=0.45: CoSA 98.9% vs CFG 83.2%) while CFG is slightly better under SOR; under transfer-with-defense, CoSA generally beats CFG and AOF, with gains around 10 points under denoising defenses. Ablation (Table III) shows the base subspace B is the dominant contributor to transfer gains (e.g. enabling only B raises PointMamba transfer ASR at eps=0.18 from 23.6% to 59.4%, vs. only S raising it to just 25.2%), with combining B+S giving the best result (62.7%). Imperceptibility (Table V) shows CoSA's CD/HD/l2 distortions are lower than CFG and PF-Attack but higher than AOF and much higher than the non-transfer-oriented 3D-Adv. Runtime (Table IV) is 10.3s per example, comparable to KNN (9.6s) and CFG (6.2s), cheaper than PF-Attack (18.0s). Swapping the autoencoder backbone from Point-MAE to PointFlow (Table VI) gives comparable average transfer ASR (55.5% vs 53.1%), suggesting the gain is attributable to the subspace formulation rather than the specific AE.
core_contribution: The paper proposes CoSA (Compact Subspace Attack), a transferable black-box adversarial attack on 3D point cloud classifiers. Its own contribution (not related work) is: (1) a 'compact subspace' reformulation of point-cloud adversarial optimization, decomposing the attack into a prototype-guided 'base subspace' B that captures class-level semantic structure and a low-rank 'perturbation subspace' S that captures coherent, architecture-agnostic variation; (2) a concrete instantiation using a frozen, pretrained Point-MAE autoencoder, per-class k-means prototype dictionaries D_y, sparse (l1-regularized) reconstruction coefficients alpha over the dictionary, and a low-rank perturbation factorization ΔD_y = U*Gamma optimized jointly under a nuclear-norm term (rank regularization on Gamma) and an orthogonality term on U; (3) an empirical demonstration that constraining perturbations to this compact subspace improves cross-architecture attack transferability relative to eight prior transferable point-cloud attacks, while remaining comparatively robust to common input-transformation defenses.
threat_model: Black-box transfer-attack setting for 3D point cloud classification: the adversary has full white-box access only to a surrogate classifier f_s and no access to the target classifier f_t (trained on the same label space but an unseen architecture). The adversary crafts an adversarial point cloud P_adv = P + delta by optimizing against f_s under an explicit geometric distortion bound (l_infinity budget, epsilon = 0.18 or 0.45, plus a Chamfer+Hausdorff perceptual loss), then evaluates whether f_t(P_adv) != y. This is the standard untargeted transferable-attack threat model used across the field, applied to safety-critical point-cloud perception (the paper cites autonomous driving / service robotics as motivating applications) rather than a novel threat model introduced by this paper.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations/scope notes: (1) CoSA's ASR is not universally best -- CFG beats it under the SOR defense, and it does not match 3D-Adv's imperceptibility (3D-Adv is explicitly not transfer-oriented, so this is an expected tradeoff). (2) The white-box (same-model) ASR for CoSA is sometimes slightly below the strongest white-box-only baselines (e.g. PointNet white-box 97.7% vs. 3D-Adv's 100.0%), reflecting the perceptibility/transferability tradeoff the authors themselves discuss. (3) Evaluation is restricted to classification on two datasets and four classifier architectures; no results are given for segmentation/detection tasks or for physical-world attacks. (4) The paper states future work will extend the framework to physical-world attacks and to robust point-cloud defenses, implying the current work does not address either. (5) Code is stated as 'to be made public upon acceptance' -- not yet released/verifiable at time of writing.

datasets_used: ScanObjectNN (11,416 train / 2,882 test, real-world indoor scans)
quantitative_results: PointNet->PointMamba transfer ASR, eps=0.45, ModelNet40: CoSA 65.6% vs CFG 56.8% (best baseline)
DGCNN->PointMamba transfer ASR, eps=0.45, ModelNet40: CoSA 75.9% vs CFG 72.5%
PointNet white-box under SRS defense, eps=0.45, ModelNet40: CoSA 98.9% vs CFG 83.2%
Ablation (Table III), PointNet->PointMamba transfer ASR eps=0.18: no B/no S 23.6%, only S 25.2%, only B 59.4%, B+S (full CoSA) 62.7%

Imperceptibility on PointNet/ModelNet40 (Table V): CoSA CD=1.749e-3, HD=1.074e-2, l2=6.816e-2, vs CFG CD=3.561e-3/HD=2.611e-2/l2=9.307e-2 and 3D-Adv CD=0.041e-3/HD=0.609e-2/l2=0.272e-2 (lowest, non-transfer method)
Per-example runtime attacking PointNet on ModelNet40 (Table IV): CoSA 10.3s vs 3D-Adv 12.8s, KNN 9.6s, PF-Attack 18.0s, CFG 6.2s
AE backbone ablation (Table VI): Point-MAE avg transfer ASR 55.5% (CD=6.6e-4, HD=4.8e-3) vs PointFlow 53.1% (CD=8.5e-4, HD=3.8e-3)

Prototype count m_y and rank r sensitivity: increasing m_y from 3->5 improves ASR then saturates/declines beyond 5 (default m_y=5); increasing r from 1->3 improves ASR then diminishing returns beyond 3 (default r=3)
